mnemonics.title: Power Sources in CMOS
mnemonics.content: Remember the 3 Ps: Switching (dynamic), Power leakage (static), and Short-circuit power - all sum up to total chip power consumption.

mnemonics.title: DVFS Benefits
mnemonics.content: Think of DVFS like a car: slow down (lower frequency) and use less fuel (lower voltage) when you don't need speed, save energy for when you do.

mnemonics.title: Power Reduction Techniques Hierarchy
mnemonics.content: Start big: Power domains → Then moderate: Clock/_frequency → Then fine: Voltage scaling - like layers of defense against power waste.

acronyms.usage: Processor power management technique that adjusts voltage and frequency based on workload demands to reduce energy consumption.
Maximum heat dissipation specification for processors that guides cooling system design and power budgeting.
Dominant transistor technology used in modern processors, known for low static power consumption.
NVIDIA's parallel computing platform enabling energy-efficient GPU-based computations.

acronyms.expansion: Giga Floating Point Operations per Watt
acronyms.usage: Standard metric for measuring processor energy efficiency, higher values indicate better efficiency.

visualTips.visualization: Picture a graph where power consumption shoots up as voltage increases - like a steep hill (P ∝ V²). Small voltage drops yield big power savings.
Imagine a city where only some buildings can have electricity at once due to limited power grid - that's dark silicon, where only portions of a chip can be active.
Think of a feedback loop: heat → more leakage → more heat → more leakage. Like a snowball rolling downhill, it keeps growing.
Picture a ladder: C0 (fully awake, highest power) at top, C6/C7 (deepest sleep, lowest power) at bottom. Climbing down saves more energy.

analogies.analogy: Like turning off the light in an empty room - no point running the switch when nobody's using it.
Like completely unplugging a device from the wall when not in use, rather than just turning it off with a remote.
Like driving a car with an automatic transmission that shifts to lower gears to save fuel when cruising, and shifts up for highway speed.
Like a smartphone that gets too hot to hold if you run all apps at maximum - the heat limits how hard you can push the processor.
Adding more people to a project has diminishing returns - the part that can't be parallelized (meetings, reviews) limits overall speedup and energy efficiency gains.
